MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 5 - Marketing analytics and artificial intelligence: Gaining customer insightsPart 2 - Marketing Research
Chapter 4 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

Learning Objectives for Lecture 5
4.3 Outline the steps in the marketing research process.

Part 2  - Marketing Research

Marketing Research (1 of 23)
Marketing research is the systematic design, collection, analysis and reporting of data relevant to a specific marketing situation facing an organisation.
Marketing research:
gives marketers insights into customer motivations, purchase behaviour and satisfaction
helps marketers to assess market potential and market share, or to measure the effectiveness of price, place, promotion and product
comprises both qualitative research and quantitative research.
Q: ‘The growing use of marketing intelligence raises a number of ethical issues.’ Can you think of some examples?

Figure 4.2
The marketing research process

Marketing Research (3 of 23)
Defining the problem and research objectives
Exploratory research is marketing research used to gather preliminary information that will help define the problem, and to suggest hypotheses.
Descriptive research is marketing research used to describe marketing problems, situations or markets.
Causal research is marketing research used to test hypotheses about cause-and-effect ry relationships.
Managers often start with exploratory research and then follow with descriptive or causal research.

Marketing Research (4 of 23)
Developing the research plan
The research plan outlines:
sources of existing data
specific research approaches and contact methods
sampling plans and instruments.
Research objectives must be translated into specific information needs.
Q: Given that market research is so important, why do you think so many companies neglect to conduct research?

Marketing Research (5 of 23)
Developing the research plan (cont.)
The research plan can call for gathering secondary data, primary data or both.
Secondary data consists of information that already exists somewhere, having been collected for another purpose.
Primary data consists of information collected for the specific purpose at hand.

Marketing Research (6 of 23)
Developing the research plan (cont.)
Gathering secondary data
Secondary data can be sourced from the company’s internal database, government sources, periodicals and books, and commercial data services.
Using commercial online databases, marketing researchers can conduct their own searches of secondary data sources, e.g.:
commercial websites
industry associations, government agencies, business publications, news media
internet search engines.

Table 4.2
Sources of secondary data (selected examples)
Internal sources: Internal sources include company profit and loss statements, balance sheets, sales figures, sales call reports, invoices, inventory records and prior research reports.
Government publications: The Year Book Australia provides summary data on the Australian economy, demography and society. A catalogue of publications is available from the Australian Bureau of Statistics and also from Australian Government Publishing Service offices in state capitals. Census data relating to population, household income and expenditure, can be accessed online from <www.abs.gov.au>.
Periodicals and books: Examples include: Business Periodicals Index; marketing journals; Australian and US trade magazines; general business newspapers and magazines published in Australia and New Zealand; US publications BusinessWeek, Fortune and Forbes; many specialist publications relating to specific industries.
Commercial data: Here are just a few of the dozens of commercial research houses selling data to subscribers:
Nielsen Company
Roy Morgan Research Centre
Specialist researchers such as the Federal Chamber of Automotive Industries and IDC (International Data Corporation
BIS Shrapnel undertakes syndicated research on the food services industry and the construction industry.
Deloitte Access Economics

Marketing Research (8 of 23)
Gathering secondary data (cont.)
The researcher must evaluate secondary information carefully to make certain it is:
relevant (fits research project needs)
accurate (reliably collected and reported)
current (up-to-date enough for current decisions)
impartial (objectively collected and reported).

Marketing Research (9 of 23)
Primary data collection
Researchers must also take great care when collecting primary data to ensure the data will be:
relevant
accurate
current
unbiased.

Marketing Research (10 of 23)
Primary data collection (cont.)
Research approaches
Observational research involves gathering primary data by observing relevant people, actions and situations.
Ethnographic research is a form of observational research that involves sending trained observers to watch and interact with consumers in their ‘natural habitats’.
Survey research is the approach best suited for gathering descriptive information.
Experimental research is the approach best suited for gathering causal information.

Table 4.3
Planning primary data collection

Q: How would a marketer of a new restaurant use observational, survey and experimental research?

Marketing Research (12 of 23)
Primary data collection (cont.)
Contact methods
Information can be collected by:
mail
telephone
personal interview
online.

Table 4.3

Marketing Research (14 of 23)
Contact methods (cont.)
Personal interviewing takes two forms:
individual interviewing: talking with people in their homes or offices, on the street or in shopping malls
group interviewing: inviting small groups of people to meet with a trained interviewer to talk about a product, service or organisation.
The interviewer ‘focuses’ the group discussion on important issues – hence the term focus group interviewing.
Q: Should people be ‘incentivised’ as a way of encouraging them to agree to be interviewed? Why, or why not?

Marketing Research (15 of 23)
Contact methods (cont.)
Online marketing research involves collecting primary data online through:
internet surveys
online panels
experiments
content analysis
online focus groups
brand communities.
Predictive analytics involves extracting information from data and using it to generate predictions for each individual customer or prospect.

Marketing Research (16 of 23)
Contact methods (cont.)
Online marketing research offers real advantages, such as:
speed
low cost
accessible to both large and small businesses.
Q: What are some other advantages of online marketing research?

Marketing Research (17 of 23)
Contact methods (cont.)
Qualitative web-based research approaches include:
online depth interviews
focus groups
blogs
social networks.
Online focus groups involve gathering a small group of people online with a trained moderator to:
chat about a product, service or organisation
gain qualitative insights about consumer attitudes and behaviours.

Marketing Research (18 of 23)
Contact methods (cont.)
Netnographic research involves monitoring online communities of interest to gain customer insights from posts to online forums, blogs, and social media generally.
Online behavioural targeting involves using online data to target ads and offers to specific consumers.
Privacy is an explosive issue facing online researchers.
Q: At what point does sophisticated online research become consumer stalking? What do you think?

Marketing Research (19 of 23)
Sampling plan
A sample is a segment of the population selected for marketing research to represent the population as a whole.
Designing the sample requires three decisions:
Sampling unit: Who is to be studied?
Sample size: How many people should be included?
Sampling procedure: How should the people in the sample be chosen?

Table 4.5
Types of samples
Probability sample
Simple random sample
Every member of the population has a known and equal chance of selection.
Stratified random sample
The population is divided into mutually exclusive groups (such as age groups) and random samples are drawn from each group.
Cluster (area) sample
The population is divided into mutually exclusive groups (such as blocks) and the researcher draws a sample of the groups to interview.
Non-probability sample
Convenience sample
The researcher selects the easiest population members from which to obtain information.
Judgment sample
The researcher uses his or her judgment to select population members who are good prospects for accurate information.
Quota sample
The researcher finds and interviews a prescribed number of people in each of several categories.

Marketing Research (21 of 23)
Research instruments
Marketing researchers have a choice of two main research instruments:
questionnaires
mechanical devices.
Q: What are some advantages and disadvantages of using (a) questionnaires and (b) mechanical devices?

Marketing Research (22 of 23)
Implementing the research plan
Putting the marketing research plan into action involves collecting, processing and analysing the information.
Data collection can be carried out by the company’s marketing research staff or by outside organisations.
Researchers must guard against problems with data collection techniques and technologies, data quality and timeliness.

Marketing Research (23 of 23)
Interpreting and reporting the findings
Interpretation should not be left only to the researchers.
Researchers are often experts in research design and statistics, but the marketing manager knows more about the problem and the decisions that must be made

Market research
Q: Given that market research is so important, why do you think so many companies neglect to conduct research?
Q: Managers think they already “know the market” and “know the customer”. Their retailer customers think they too “know the customer”. Their sales reps only know the customers they sell to, not the competitors’ customers. Some managers might also think that it is a sign of weakness/lack of knowledge to ask for market research.

Market research
Q: How would a marketer of a new restaurant use observational, survey and experimental research?
A: Observation: study what competing restaurants are doing, how customers walk down the street and choose a restaurant, how they are greeted, and served by staff.
Survey: survey customers in the area regarding their reasons for dining out, price-range they want to pay, type of cuisine, hours convenient to them for dining and so on.
Experiment: add and delete items on the menu, change prices, change messages in the window.

Market research
Q: Should people be ‘incentivised’ as a way of encouraging them to agree to be interviewed? Why, or why not?
A: Pros – incentives might attract more participants they incentives and a way of thanking participants. Cons - However, incentives might attract people who want the incentive but are not really the target/sample you need, and so the information they provide might not reflect the views of your “real” intended sample.

Market research
A: Can access customers who would not otherwise make themselves available for research or cannot be located.
A: In my opinion it becomes stalking when (1) customer data is used to intrude into what that customer might consider to be private matters, such as their income, budgeting, or more-”personal” purchases and (2) when customers are repeatedly contacted after they have said thatthey do not want to be contacted.
Q: What are some other advantages of online marketing research? (In addition to speed, low cost and accessible to both large and small businesses.
Q: At what point does sophisticated online research become consumer stalking? What do you think?
